data.title: Franklin Global Equity Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: The second quarter of 2025 began with US President Donald Trump's announcement of "reciprocal" tariffs that were more sweeping than many had expected, leading to significant financial market volatility. However, global trade tensions eased during the rest of the quarter as the United States delayed planned tariff hikes, reducing investor fears of a global recession.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in the US, especially in Health Care and Energy, as well as stock selection in the UK were leading contributors.
  • Detractors: Stock selection detracted from relative return in Japan and continental Europe. Region and sector allocation decisions also detracted.
  • Outlook: Leading indicators of economic growth appear to have strengthened during the past month, suggesting that the global economy may avoid a recession. However, significant trade and geopolitical uncertainty remains, and has yet to be fully reflected in asset prices. Equity markets are likely to become choppier in the near term as factors such as energy price volatility and tariff deadlines increase the possibility of corrections.

Performance Review

  • Stock selection was mixed at the region and sector level but added value overall, especially in the US led by the Health Care sector. Selection detracted in US Information Technology. Selection also added value in the UK but detracted in Japan and continental Europe. Stock selection has little impact in other regions.
  • Region and sector allocation decisions detracted, especially the overweight to the UK and a small allocation to Emerging Markets which are not in the benchmark.
  • In the US, the benefit of an underweight to Energy was more than offset by the negative impact of an overweight to Health Care.

Outlook

  • Leading indicators of economic growth appear to have strengthened during the past month, suggesting that the global economy may avoid a recession. However, significant trade and geopolitical uncertainty remains, and has yet to be fully reflected in asset prices.
  • While investors seemed to look through the impact of the Israel-Iran conflict in the Middle East, a continuation or broadening of the conflict could also increase market volatility.
  • Hard US economic data for employment, growth and inflation looks resilient, and the US tax bill will likely give some support to the US economy offsetting any drag on US growth from tariffs. This upturn in data supports the market's rally through June-end, but is offset by current valuations and elevated sentiment.
  • Equity markets are likely to become choppier in the near term as factors such as energy price volatility and tariff deadlines increase the possibility of corrections. In addition, while investors seemed to look through the impact of the Israel-Iran conflict in the Middle East, a continuation or broadening of the conflict could also increase market volatility.
  • Against this background, we are confident that investors will continue to reward stocks that are attractively valued and invest in companies that have solid growth prospects and the ability to generate cash for their shareholders. We believe that our well-diversified, disciplined strategy may serve investors well in this environment.

Holding Fund

Morningstar Rating™: Source: Morningstar®, 06/30/2025. For each mutual fund and ETF with at least a 3-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ based on how a fund ranks on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure against other funds in the same category. This measure takes into account variations in a fund's monthly performance, and does not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund's Class A shares received a Morningstar Rating of 5, 5 and 4 and fund's Class I shares received a Morningstar Rating of 5, 5 and 5 star(s) for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Franklin Templeton provides this fund's Morningstar Rating™ for Class A and Class I shares only. Other share classes may have different Morningstar ratings.

data.description: The following slide deck was published by Colliers International Group Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Franklin Gold And Precious Metals Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Advisor Class: FGADX Class A: FKRCX Commentary | as of June 30, 2025

Key Takeaways

  • Precious and Base Metals Markets: In the second quarter of 2025 (2Q25), a combination of aggressive US trade policy, geopolitical tensions and military conflicts, ongoing inflationary concerns, central bank buying and US dollar devaluation (-7.0% versus other major currencies, to a three-year low) supported a 5.7% gain in gold prices, which ended June at $3,303 per troy ounce. While these conditions have pushed the metal 25.9% higher so far this year, gold's rally lost momentum in May and June, when bullion mostly traded in the $3,300 to $3,400 range. Gold's "haven" demand eventually eased amid a combination of improving economic sentiment, milder-than-expected inflation reports, signs of progress in trade talks and easing tensions in the Middle East. Silver, platinum and palladium all fared better than gold as they posted respective quarterly gains of 5.9% (to $36.11 per ounce), 36.2% (to $1,359) and 11.7% (to $1,107), with most of the rise occurring in June. In the global base metals complex, the London Metal Exchange (LME) Index, which tracks prices on benchmark three-month futures contracts for copper, aluminum, nickel, tin, lead and zinc, was negatively impacted by lingering economic growth concerns yet increased 0.8% in 2Q25 as it gradually pulled out of an April slump. Prices among these major industrial metals were split, with the quarterly gains for LME copper, aluminum and lead mostly offset by the declines in nickel, tin and zinc. The 1.6% rise in in LME copper prices was fueled by a sharp decline in warehouse stockpiles, which in late June were at the lowest levels since August 2023.

  • Contributors: Off-benchmark portfolio exposure to companies that produce industrial metals and non-gold precious metals, plus a long list of off-index or overweighted gold industry stocks that topped the benchmark return.

  • Detractors: Individual stocks that pared the fund's narrow measure of relative outperformance in the gold industry.

  • Outlook: Record-high gold prices delivered robust cash generation in many gold miners' first-quarter financial reports, and we believe significantly higher 2Q25 prices should amplify these results.

Top Equity Issuers (% of Total)
Holding Fund
NEWMONT CORP (NEM) 4.61
G MINING VENTURES CORP (OTCPK:GMINF) 4.04
AGNICO EAGLE MINES LTD (AEM) 3.68
ALAMOS GOLD INC (AGI) 3.60
BARRICK MINING CORP (B) 3.07
ANGLOGOLD ASHANTI PLC (AU) 2.95
PERSEUS MINING LTD (OTCPK:PMNXF) 2.91
ORLA MINING LTD (ORLA) 2.87
GREATLAND RESOURCES LTD (OTCPK:GRLG.F) 2.76
TOREX GOLD RESOURCES INC (OTCPK:TORXF) 2.65
Sector Allocation (% of Total)
Sector Fund
Long Life Gold Mines 44.51
Gold Exploration & Development 24.56
Medium Life Gold Mines 11.80
Diversified Metals & Mining 9.06
Precious Metals & Minerals 4.09
Silver 3.48
Copper 0.70
Other 0.52
Cash & Cash Equivalents 1.30

Performance Review

  • Compared to the broader stock market, gold-mining equities collectively outperformed as many investors sought defensive havens amid volatility driven by US tariff and debt concerns. Gold equities also fared much better than physical gold's quarterly performance. Within the materials sector, gold producers were standouts as they generally delivered strong 1Q25 financial results, with senior gold companies reporting record cash flows, operational efficiencies and stabilizing production costs. In broad terms, gold miners' modest valuations and high profit margins made them particularly compelling to many investors compared to other materials subsectors, several of which faced challenges like oversupply or weaker demand. Junior gold miners generally outperformed their established large-capitalization peers given their higher leverage to gold price movements, though in some cases their gains were tempered by financing, geopolitical or operational risks.

  • The fund outperformed its benchmark in 2Q25 due to favorable industry allocation decisions, while stock selection was a minor hindrance. Aside from our core gold holdings (comprising 80.87% of the portfolio at June-end), five out of six smaller, off-benchmark industry exposures posted gains that in some cases more than doubled or tripled the FTSE Gold Mines Index's advance. In general, we saw robust returns from junior (small-cap) and mid-tier gold producers as well as companies outside the gold industry that focus on silver, copper and platinum/palladium mining. Among the gold miners, several key contributors were classified as exploration or pre-production status.

  • On an individual security basis, all of the top-10 relative contributors were in the gold industry, where a long list of off-index or overweighted holdings outpaced the benchmark (including a couple that rose more than 100%), led by Greatland Resources, Southern Cross Gold (OTCPK:MWSNF), Skeena Resources (SKE), Artemis Gold (OTCPK:ARGTF), Onyx Gold (OTCQX:ONXGF) and Montage Gold (MAUTF). The fund also benefited from strategic underweightings in large, geographically diversified gold producers that lagged the index, including Agnico Eagle Mines, Barrick Mining (formerly Barrick Gold), Northern Star Resources (OTCPK:NESRF) and Gold Fields (GFI).

  • Conversely, the key detractors were focused on individual stocks that pared the fund's narrow measure of relative outperformance in the gold industry. A notable hindrance in this regard was our lack of exposure to Lundin Gold (OTCQX:LUGDF), Kinross Gold (KGC), Coeur Mining (CDE) and a few others in the FTSE Gold Index that traded significantly higher. Among the fund's active investments, there were 2Q25 losses for its off-benchmark or overweighted positions in Ora Banda Mining (OTC:ESGFF), G Mining Ventures, Calibre Mining (merged with Equinox Gold (EQX) by period-end), Black Cat Syndicate (BLCAF), Ascot Resources (OTCQX:AOTVF), Catalyst Metals (OTCPK:CTYMF), Vault Minerals (OTCPK:REDLF) and other, lesser detractors.

  • A substantial investment in Newmont (NEM) was the top contributor in absolute terms as it increased by roughly 20%, but it was also the biggest relative detractor as we kept only about a quarter of the index's 19.7% average weighting.

Outlook

  • Gold prices averaged $3,289 per ounce in 2Q25, compared to $2,858 per ounce in 1Q25 and $2,660 in 4Q24, thereby extending the positive momentum we saw over the previous four quarters. Although we think current cost inflation and skills shortages in the broader mining industry may continue to weigh on companies' operating costs to some degree, many of those pressures have been easing in the near term, and we believe higher gold prices should more than offset any cost pressures, allowing many companies to deliver potentially meaningful earnings and free cash flow growth. As a result, we remain constructive on gold and continue to see what we consider attractive valuations across the industry. Rapidly increasing cash flow from gold producers also leaves us optimistic about further industry consolidation in the coming months.

  • Although we believe the US administration's "reciprocal" tariff plan should have limited direct impact on most gold companies as bullion was excluded from tariffs, the volatility following related announcements—along with lingering uncertainty as implementation dates continue to get pushed out—has weighed on global economic growth. In this environment, we are staying focused on company fundamentals, and looking for opportunities with any pullbacks, but also being mindful of company pitfalls such as excessive debt.

Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (A) for Advisor Class periods prior to 12/31/1996, a restated figure is used based on the fund's Class A performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (B) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. To the extent the portfolio invests in a concentration of certain securities, regions or industries, it is subject to increased volatility. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Small- and mid-cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. The portfolio is non-diversified and may invest in a relatively small number of issuers, which may negatively impact the fund's performance and result in greater fluctuation in the value of the fund's shares. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund's prospectus.

Glossary

Free cash flow (FCF) is a measure of financial performance calculated as operating cash flow minus capital expenditures.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager's assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund's portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

data.title: Canadian Utilities LimitedA 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Canadian Utilities LimitedA in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Portfolios Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: U.S. equities preserved through tariff and geopolitical scares to deliver solid gains in the second quarter. The broad market S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index returned 10.9%, swinging from a near-bear market to all-time high. The benchmark Russell 1000 Growth Index did even better as risk-on sentiment took hold, rising 17.8%, and outperforming the Russell 1000 Value Index by over 1,400 basis points.
  • Contributors: An underweight to Apple as well as holdings in Netflix, Eaton (ETN) and Meta Platforms (META). Stock selection in the communication services and industrials; an underweight to consumer staples.
  • Detractors: Holdings in UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) and Visa (V) as well as an underweight to Broadcom (AVGO). Stock selection in health care and information technology (IT); an overweight to health care and underweight to IT.
  • Outlook: While the S&P 500 has broadened, the Russell 1000 Growth Index remains dominated by its eight $1 trillion+ market cap components. We remain confident and committed to our more balanced, risk-aware approach to growth investing, seeking to add value through differentiated stock selection.

Top Equity Issuers (% of Total)

Holding Portfolio
NVIDIA Corp (NVDA) 10.58
Amazon (AMZN).com Inc 8.12
Microsoft Corp (MSFT) 7.76
Meta Platforms Inc 6.96
Visa Inc 4.83
Apple Inc (AAPL) 4.73
Netflix Inc (NFLX) 4.51
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) Inc 2.30
Intuit (INTU) Inc 2.27
Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) Inc 2.25

Performance Review

  • The Portfolios' more diversified and defensive exposure compared to our concentrated benchmark reversed from a tailwind in the first quarter to a headwind in the second. We were disappointed with the weak quarterly performance of several of our more countercyclical holdings. UnitedHealth Group saw a renewed selloff following a first-quarter earnings miss and guidance reduction as the managed care company announced elevated cost pressure and the exit of its CEO.
  • Thermo Fisher Scientific lagged due to concerns around spending cuts in the academic/government and biopharma segments due to regulatory fears and tariffs. Visa was hurt by Senate approval of the GENIUS Act, a bill aimed at regulating stablecoins that raises the risk of disruption to Visa's traditional payments model and its valuation being at the high end of its historical range.
  • Mega cap positioning was mostly neutral as an overweight to Meta and underweight to underperforming Apple offset relative headwinds from underweights to Microsoft and new addition Broadcom.
  • Netflix in communication services, one of the Portfolios' largest active weights, saw its shares rise due to overall continued robust execution with double-digit revenue growth, driven by a balance of subscriber growth and price, and continued margin expansion. We remain confident in the company's long-term strategy, strong market position and attractiveness of the global streaming market.
  • Industrials also did well, buoyed by stocks tied to power and electrification, like out-of-benchmark portfolio holding Eaton.
  • In IT, we trimmed Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) to manage the risk amid intensified geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and China, directing the proceeds into purchases of AI-indexed chip makers Broadcom and Marvell Technology (MRVL).
  • The additions of commercial airline manufacturer Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) and industrials gases provider Linde (LIN) helped diversify the portfolio beyond traditional growth areas and prepare for wider large cap leadership.
  • Our sales targeted companies whose end markets have become further challenged due to the broader macro backdrop – mass market retailer Target (TGT) and contract research organization ICON (ICLR) — or technological challenges in the AI era – software maker Adobe (ADBE).

Sector Allocation (% of Total)

Sector Portfolio
Information Technology 38.78
Communication Services 13.72
Consumer Discretionary 12.18
Industrials 9.35
Financials 9.18
Health Care 8.74
Materials 2.40
Real Estate 1.41
Consumer Staples 1.36
Cash & Cash Equivalents 2.88

Outlook

  • Boosted by AI enthusiasm along with an easing of volatility and a renewed embrace of risk taking, IT finished the second quarter at 49% of the Russell 1000 Growth Index (RLG), the second-highest concentration since parts of the sector were reclassified in 2018 and 2023. While the S&P 500 Index has shown signs of broadening as judged by the advance/decline line and the number of stocks closing above their 50-day moving average, the RLG remains dominated by its eight $1 trillion+ market cap components.
  • Our longtime IT underweight specifically, and our sector allocations more generally, are a function of managing concentration and industry risk and striving to maintain a portfolio with a diversified set of growth drivers. To this end, the long-term success of the Portfolios will always be guided by fundamental stock selection. The second quarter is a reminder that there will be periods and market conditions when the Portfolios will be challenged in keeping pace with the benchmark. We remain confident and committed to our more balanced, risk-aware approach to growth investing, seeking to add value through sector and stock selection investing in differentiated, well-positioned companies.

ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Portfolios Average annual total returns (%) - as of June 30, 2025-PRELIMINARY

data.title: U.S. Tariffs Doing Real Damage To Japanese Profit Margins
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

Strong exports to EU and ASEAN offset US, China drop

Japanese exports rose modestly in the first half of 2025 amid strong growth in shipments to the EU and Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) economies. At the end of 2024, the US (20%), China (18%), ASEAN (14%), and the EU (9%) were Japan’s top trading partners. This year, we’ve seen strong export growth to ASEAN and the EU, while shipments to the US and China declined. By item, chemicals (1.6%), machinery (3.4%), electrical machinery (1.6%), and motor vehicles (1.4%) all rose. Yet, iron, steel and auto part exports dropped quite sharply by 10.2% and 6.3%, respectively.

Exports to the EU and ASEAN rose in 1H25

Japan exports fluctuating significantly amid tariff-related noise

Exports increased by 3.6% year-to-date in the first half of the year, even as growth rates turned negative towards mid-year. We observe that US tariff-related noise created monthly volatility during the first half. This was somewhat expected, as front-loading boosted first-quarter exports but pulled forward demand that might've shown up in the second quarter. Also, we see clear weakness for exports to the US, which dropped -0.8%.

Japan exports rose in 1H25 but exports to US contracted

Auto exports rose in volume terms

Japan's primary export is transportation equipment, mainly cars. In 1H25, car exports increased by 0.6% in value and 1.5% in volume. In the US, volumes rose 5.1%, outpacing the rest of the world (0.3%). But values dropped 3.4% compared to a 3.0% rise elsewhere. This suggests that automakers frontloaded their products and lowered prices to offset tariffs.

With the 15% tariff deal, we believe that car exports are likely to stabilise in 2H25. But it’s still uncertain whether they will continue to sacrifice profits or pass rising costs onto consumers.

We also find a similar trend of cutting prices on other exports like computers and medical products. We believe that this could threaten wage growth in the coming years. We’ve seen solid wage growth over the past three years, partly due to strong corporate earnings. Thus, this could cloud Japan's ability to generate sustainable wage growth.

Auto exports: value vs volume

Semiconductor-related exports grew strongly

Chip-making equipment and semiconductors rose 4.3% and 5.5%, respectively. These two account for about 10% of total exports. This growth is likely due to tariff exemptions and substantial AI investments. Similar trends are seen in Taiwan, South Korea, and Malaysia.

Despite US onshoring efforts in semiconductors, it seems investment inflows haven’t increased. Japan, a leader in device technology, is vital for the health of the global chip-making industry. However, exports to the US declined, while shipments to the rest of the world grew. Semiconductor exports showed a similar pattern: weak exports to the US likely result from supply chains being concentrated in Asia, rather than tariffs.

Also, Japanese exports to Taiwan decreased significantly in the second quarter, primarily because most Japanese chips are designed for general use rather than high-end applications. High-end chips are typically assembled and manufactured in Taiwan. Therefore, export trends may vary by product segment compared to other chip manufacturers.

We believe that tariff exemptions have increased global chip exports in 1H25 as companies seek to avoid possible sectoral tariffs. Today, the US announced 20% tariffs for Taiwan. We don’t know the specifics on chips yet. But higher levies on Taiwan will have a broader negative impact on semiconductors globally in 2H25.

Most chip related exports should be inter-regional exports

Conclusion: Tariff impact hasn't yet been fully passed on to consumers

Japanese data indicate that US tariffs have had an overall negative effect on exports. The impact was particularly significant for goods facing sector-specific tariffs, such as iron, steel, and automobiles. This may present challenges for pharmaceutical and semiconductor exports if tariffs are imposed on these items in the future. For cars, computers, and medical products, Japanese exporters appeared to have offset some tariff impacts by reducing prices. But they may eventually pass costs on to consumers, potentially causing delayed price pressures in the US.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Figma's Meteoric Rise Creates Big Risks For You
data.description:
Seeing a 10% or 20% increase in share price in the course of a single day is incredibly rare in the market for any particular business. So you can imagine just how rare it is to see a firm skyrocket by over 200%. That was the case regarding software giant Figma (NYSE:FIG), which finally priced its shares and began trading publicly for the first time on July 31st. The company went public at a price of $33 per share. By the end of the day, the stock was up to $115.50. That's an increase of 250% during the trading day. Unless you happen to be an insider of the business, or unless you were in a unique position to get some of the shares that were offered right out of the gate, you likely missed out on most or all of this upside. But the big question remains of whether additional upside is possible from here.

Fundamentally speaking, Figma appears to be an interesting business. Its financials are frankly all over the place, driven in large part by the $1 billion termination fee it got from Adobe (ADBE) in 2023. But one thing that we can say for sure is that this is a rapidly growing business in a very attractive market. Unfortunately, I fear as though market participants are pricing the company into the stratosphere. With a market capitalization as of this writing of $48.24 billion, the business is trading at levels that, to me, frankly don't make sense. I have no problem believing that the enterprise will continue to grow nicely for the foreseeable future. But given how expensive shares are at the moment, I believe that assigning the firm a soft ‘sell’ rating is appropriate.

A fascinating business at an astronomical price

I don't think anybody will disagree with me when I say that the Internet has created some remarkable opportunities the likes of which the world has never seen before. It might be the greatest wealth generation machine since the establishment of the stock market in the Netherlands in 1602. And over the last few decades now, major companies that now have market capitalizations that significantly eclipse the industrial giants of the past serve as the largest publicly traded companies the world has ever known. Given the network effects that exist there, I fully expect this trend to continue. And one of the companies at the forefront of this continued technological revolution is Figma.

The company began operations back in 2012. At the time, it started off as a browser-based tool for designing user interfaces. Since then, it has expanded into a major software-oriented enterprise dedicated to helping teams take their ideas and turn them into products. This has taken in the form of an entire ecosystem dedicated to helping people ideate, visualize, build, and even ship out their own creative concepts. There are multiple examples of this. For instance, in 2021, the company created FigJam, which serves as an online whiteboard for teams to be able to brainstorm and ideate together. In 2023, they launched Dev Mode, which is a dedicated space on the platform to help teams more efficiently translate design concepts into coded products. Figma Design is focused more on free form design exploration.

Last year, the company launched Figma Slides, which serves as a product for designers and their collaborators to build out better designed and more collaborative presentations. This year, they have launched four additional products, which are Figma Sites, Figma Make, Figma Buzz, and Figma Draw. These help with all sorts of things, including helping you to design a website and directly publish it online with your own URL, helping to create social media assets, display ads, and more, and aiding with editing content that you make.

Management has achieved remarkable traction over the years. According to the firm, the platform boasts 13 million monthly active users. Its customer base includes 450,000 paid users and consists of 95% of the Fortune 500 companies that are out there. In fact, the company counts 78% of the Forbes 2000 as its customers. If management is correct about the opportunity that exists here, there could be a lot of growth over the next several years. By moving heavily into AI and focusing on apps, the company believes that it can expand into a market that they estimate to be worth $33 billion. By the year 2028, it's expected that there will be 1 billion new apps in existence compared to today. And by 2029, an estimated 144 million people globally will work in the space that would benefit from the firm's services. That's nearly half of the total population of the US. Already, the market is quite large. Global spending on software this year alone is estimated to exceed $1.2 trillion.

Usually when I dig into a company the first time, I like to see at least three years of operating results. But management has only made available two years and the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Even so, during this short window of time, financial performance has been robust. From 2023 to 2024, revenue expanded nicely from $504.9 million to $749 million. This came as the number of paid customers that generate in excess of $10,000 in annual recurring revenue grew from 7,233 to 10,517, with the number generating more than $100,000 per year rising from 630 to 963.

Even though the trajectory for revenue during this time was positive, financial performance was all over the map. The firm went from a net profit of $737.8 million in 2023 to a net loss of $732.1 million last year. This massive swing can be attributed to the $1 billion termination fee that the company received from its failed merger with Adobe in December of 2023. This unfortunately has distorted results for the 2023 fiscal year, making them pretty much unrepresentative of the health of the business as a whole. For instance, operating cash flow plummeted from $1.05 billion to negative $61.7 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $766.4 million to $208.2 million. On the other hand, EBITDA, which excludes this termination fee, and is able to avoid the complex tax implications of it, went from negative $53.6 million to positive $92.6 million.

For the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, revenue for the business totaled $228.2 million. That's 46.1% above the $156.2 million the company reported the previous year. This came as the number of paid customers bringing in more than $10,000 in annual recurring revenue for the company expanded from 8,007 to 11,107 and as the number bringing in over $100,000 grew from 701 to 1,031.

This growth is certainly showing some positive outcomes. Net profits shot up from $13.5 million in the first quarter of 2024 to $44.9 million in the first quarter of this year. Operating cash flow went from negative $18.1 million to positive $97.2 million. The adjusted figure for this shot up from $19.4 million to $58.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $18.2 million to $46.2 million.

If we assume that the rest of 2025 will look like the first quarter did on a year over year basis, which might be a stretch considering how rapid the profit and cash flow growth would be implied to be, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $628.9 million and EBITDA of $235.1 million. Even under this rather bullish assessment, we would have a company that is trading at remarkably high levels. Using the $101.60 that shares were trading at as I conducted my research for the article, this would imply incredibly high trading multiples as the chart above illustrates.

Of course, investors who are bullish about the firm might point out that future growth is worth multiples of this nature. I don't think I would agree with that. To put this in perspective, in the table below, I project it out what the overall financial performance of the business might be under what I consider to be quite bullish assumptions. In the chart above, you can see the adjusted operating cash flow margin and the EBITDA margin for the company for 2024 and for the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year. For my growth assumptions, I am assuming margins of 35% and 30%, respectively. That table also assumes that overall revenue grows at a rate of 40% annually for the timeframe covered. That compares to the 48.3% growth achieved from 2023 to 2024 and the 46.1% achieved for the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year.

Of course, the picture can always be different than this. In my graduate level finance courses, one thing that one of my professors loved to tell us was that the one thing that we know about every financial projection is that it will be wrong. So in the first table below, I redid the calculations under the assumption that revenue will grow at a rate of 50% annually. And in the table below that, I assumed a 60% annual growth rate. Considering the large share of major customers that the business already has, I think it's going to be hard to grow at rates like this for the next few years. But even if it does, it will really take until around 2028 for shares to be even decently priced given current valuation.

Takeaway

In my personal opinion, Figma is a fascinating business. Management has achieved something remarkable with the firm. However, I do think that the market has gotten overly enthusiastic. Financial performance has been solid recently, and growth should continue in the long run. But given how the stock is currently priced, I think that assigning it a soft ‘sell’ rating is appropriate.

data.title: Roku: Immense Cash Flow Growth As Company Keeps Its Streaming Crown
data.description:
As we move deeper through the Q2 earnings season and the S&P 500 fights to justify its fresh records, it has become quite evident that investors are nervous at all-time market highs, and even companies that have reported strong "beat and raise" quarters have seen modest share price declines post-earnings.

Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU) is one of these stocks. The streaming box leader reported better-than-expected growth in Q2 alongside a vast consensus-defying guidance raise, and yet, the stock declined modestly in after-hours trading following the print. We're not exactly shedding tears for Roku, whose stock is up ~25% since the start of the year: but for long term oriented investors, this dip presents yet another buying opportunity in this iconic brand.

I wrote a buy article on Roku in May, when the stock was still trading at $73 per share. Since then, Roku has rebounded sharply (gaining far more than the rest of the stock market), but at the same time, the company has also more than justified its gains in its recent Q2 earnings release. Though Roku's valuation multiples are now tipping on the edge of expensive, I am a long-term holder of this stock and am confident in Roku's ability to keep boosting its margins and bottom line as its advertising business matures. I remain at a buy rating here post-earnings.

To me, these are the core reasons to remain bullish on Roku:

  • As the #1 streaming OS, Roku continues to benefit from secular tailwinds in streaming. Forget the streaming wars: Roku will win no matter which platform, Netflix (NFLX) or Disney (DIS) or another player, ends up retaining the most customers. Roku's operating system has maintained the #1 market share among streaming TV boxes in the U.S., and a growing number of paid subscriptions are now billed through Roku, giving Roku a cut of subscription fees.
  • Growing advertising complexity. Roku continues to benefit not only from media spending shifting from linear TV and into streaming (a trend that adtech companies like The Trade Desk (TTD) repeatedly emphasize and capitalize on), but the company is also making continuous improvements to make its platform more attractive to advertisers. The company recently released a product called Roku Ads Manager aimed at helping SMBs organize and execute their ad spending on Roku.
  • Device refreshes keep Roku relevant. Roku recently refreshed its best-selling streaming box and TVs in the spring. The company's new models are significantly smaller than the prior generation and have greater power efficiency. Not only has Roku captured strong reviews for these products, but BOM improvements have also led to significant gross margin gains.
  • Free cash flow growth. Alongside margin expansion and the continued strength of platform revenue, Roku has also achieved significant free cash flow gains that bolster a cash-rich balance sheet.

Of course, after this year's rally, Roku also trades at a premium - but not an outlandish one, in my view. At current share prices near $94, Roku trades at a market cap of $13.81 billion. After we net off the $2.25 billion of cash on the company's most recent balance sheet, Roku's resulting enterprise value is $11.56 billion.

For FY25, as shown in the chart below, Roku has boosted its full-year guidance to $4.65 billion in revenue (+13% y/y growth, and 2 points better than a prior 11% y/y growth outlook), with a bump to adjusted EBITDA to boot: the company's $375 million expectations now represent a 8.1% margin, versus 7.7% previously.

Meanwhile, for next year FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting Roku to hit $5.12 billion in revenue (+12% y/y growth). If we assume that Roku can continue at least 100bps of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to a 9.1% margin (more modest than its current pace of margin expansion, at 250bps y/y in the most recent quarter), adjusted EBITDA would be $466 million (+24% y/y). This puts Roku's valuation multiples at:

  • 2.5x EV/FY25 revenue and 2.3x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 30.8x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA and 24.8x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

We'll emphasize this point again: Roku is not a value stock. But at the same time, with the company achieving significant gains in adjusted EBITDA (Q2 EBITDA grew at a 79% y/y clip), we also think a mid-20s multiple against FY26 EBITDA still leaves broad room for upside here, especially as Roku continues to gain leverage on gross margins.

Stay long here and use any post-earnings dip as a buying opportunity.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Roku's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Roku's revenue grew 15% y/y to $1.11 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $1.07 billion (+11% y/y) by a vast four-point margin. Roku's revenue growth also largely kept pace with 16% y/y growth in Q1, which is impressive considering macroeconomic headwinds. In fact, we note that platform revenue growth of 18% y/y (which is what investors are mainly focused on for Roku) accelerated one point over 17% growth in Q1.

The company's growth in platform revenue was driven by a number of tailwinds. The first is the fact that streaming services have all raised their prices: Netflix (NFLX), for example, hiked its Standard plan to $17.99, up 16% from a previous $15.49 cost. Last year in June, meanwhile, HBO raised its standard ad-free plan cost to $16.99, from $15.99 previously.

As a reminder, customers who sign up for paid subscriptions through their Roku apps also have their subscriptions billed by Roku - for which Roku takes a cut of the subscription revenue. Roku enjoys the same kind of "walled garden" fee levies on content that has long been a heated debate in Apple's (AAPL) billing practices for the App Store. Roku has the benefit of being platform-agnostic: as the streaming companies raise prices (and we note that it is also unusual for HBO to sit under Netflix's price, so we expect hikes to continue), Roku will benefit as well.

On advertising, Roku has also made a number of product updates to streamline the ad buying process for advertisers. As previously mentioned, the company rolled out new tools to attract more SMB advertisers, making it easier to upload creative content and actionable Shopify integrations. In June, the company also announced a partnership with Amazon (AMZN) to make ad-targeting more precise for users on the Amazon DSP (ad-buying platform), particularly by ensuring more unique viewers and reducing the number of times the same person is served by the same ad.

On the profitability front, we note that Roku's overall gross margins improved 90bps y/y to 44.8%. The company's latest lineup of devices, as previously mentioned, are smaller and more power-efficient than the prior generation, allowing the company to shrink its BOM cost and improve device gross margins.

Roku has also exercised careful cost controls, with opex growing only 5% y/y despite supporting 15% growth in revenue. As a result, adjusted EBITDA soared 79% y/y to $78.2 million, representing a 7.0% margin that improved 250bps y/y.

The company's free cash flow story is equally impressive, with FCF of $392.0 million in the trailing twelve months growing 23% y/y over the prior-year period:

Key takeaways

Roku is sitting on multiple catalysts that are helping to boost its platform revenue (increased streaming sign-ups and higher prices, a more compelling advertising interface), while the company has also proven the consistency of its margin expansion and adjusted EBITDA/FCF growth. Though clearly not a value stock, I think Roku has less correction risk than large-cap tech stocks in the S&P 500 thanks to the company's rapid current pace of bottom-line expansion. Stay long here post-earnings.
